Plots of Functions of Several Variables The plane ax + by + cz = d z I In Engineering and Science, we often need to deal with complicated systems that depend on many variables. For example, the ideal gas equation relates the pressure P of d a gas in a container as a function of the temperature T of c

the gas and the volume V of the container: T P (T, V ) = k , (k is a constant). d V 0 b y I Recall that the graph of a function of one variable d y = f (x) is a curve in two-dimensional space R2 . The a graph of a function of two variables z = f (x, y) is a surface in three-dimensional space R3 . x I The co-ordinate axes x, y, z form a right-handed system.

Summary Partial Differentiation I The co-ordinate axes x, y, z form a right-handed system. I If y = f (x) where f : R ‚Üí R, then y is the dependent variable, and x is the independent variable. The I ax + by + cz = d is a plane. derivative, dy/dx or f ‚Ä≤ (x), is the rate of change of y with respect to x. I (x ‚ąí x0 )2 + (y ‚ąí y0 )2 + (z ‚ąí z0 )2 = a2 is a sphere centred I If z = f (x, y) where f : R2 ‚Üí R, then z is the dependent at (x0 , y0 , z0 ) with radius a. variable, and x and y are the independent variables. I z = c(x2 + y 2 ) is a paraboloid of revolution. I If we treat y as a constant and differentiate with respect p to x we obtain ‚ąāf /‚ąāx, the partial derivative of f with I z = c x2 + y 2 is a cone with semi-vertical respect to x. This number is the rate of change of z in angle tan‚ąí1 (1/c). the x direction (holding y fixed). I If we treat x as a constant and differentiate with respect I If a variable is missing in the equation, then the function to y we obtain ‚ąāf /‚ąāy, the partial derivative of f with is independent of this variable. Extend the two respect to y. This number is the rate of change of z in dimensional curve to infinity in this direction. the y direction (holding x fixed). . . Example 13 The Chain Rule(s) Let z = f (x, y) = x2 sin y + x3 y + y 5 . Find I A common situation is that z is a function of x and y, 2 2 2 2 ‚ąā z ‚ąā z ‚ąā z ‚ąā z with x and y themselves functions of other variables, , , , . ‚ąāx2 ‚ąāy 2 ‚ąāx‚ąāy ‚ąāy‚ąāx say u and v. Then z is also a function of u and v, and it ‚ąāz makes sense to ask the question ‚ÄúWhat is ?‚ÄĚ ‚ąāu I The chain rule enables us to answer this question without actually ever having to calculate z as an explicit function of u and v. It is usually simpler to use the chain rule than to differentiate directly the compositive function

w(u, v) := z(x(u, v), y(u, v)).

I Three versions of the chain rule are shown below. However, all you need to remember is that you just apply the single-variable chain rule to each argument of z. . .  Revision: single-variable chain rule Chain rule I If z = f (x) and x = x(t), then dz dz dx If z = f (x, y), x = x(t) and y = y(t), then = . dt dx dt dz ‚ąāz dx ‚ąāz dy = + . dt ‚ąāx dt ‚ąāy dt

dx x ‚ąāz dx dz dt ‚ąāx dt dx t x z t z dy ‚ąāz y dt ‚ąāy

Show that I It can sometimes be convenient to approximate a  2  2 complicated function by a polynomial. ‚ąāw ‚ąāw ‚ąāw ‚ąāw = ‚ąí . ‚ąāx ‚ąāy ‚ąāu ‚ąāv I For example, when |x| is small,

x3 sin x ‚Čą x ‚ąí , 3! x2 x4 cos x ‚Čą 1 ‚ąí + , 2! 4! x x3 x4 ex ‚Čą 1 + x + + + , 2! 3! 4! x2 x3 x4 log(1 + x) ‚Čą x ‚ąí + ‚ąí . 2 3 4 I In each of the above examples, the RHS is a Taylor polynomial for the LHS, expanded about x = 0. . .

Example 22 Definition 21 Let f (x) = ex . The Taylor polynomial of degree n for a function f (x) about a 1. Find the Taylor polynomial Pn (x) for f about the is point a = 0. X n f (k) (a) 2. For n = 1, 2, 3, sketch the graph of Pn on the Pn (x) = (x ‚ąí a)k k! interval [‚ąí1, 1]. k=0

‚Ä≤ f (n) (a) = f (a) + f (a)(x ‚ąí a) + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ + (x ‚ąí a)n . n!

I It can be shown that f (x) ‚Čą Pn (x) when x is close to a. The higher the degree n, the better the approximation. I The infinite sum obtained by sending n ‚Üí ‚ąě is called the Taylor series of f (x) about a.

. .   Functions of two variables 5 Definition 23 The Taylor polynomial of degree n for f (x, y) about (a, b) is

Pn (x, y) X k   1 X k n ‚ąākf = (a, b) (x ‚ąí a)k‚ąíj (y ‚ąí b)j k=0 k! j=0 j ‚ąāxk‚ąíj ‚ąāy j

X n X k ‚ąākf (x ‚ąí a)k‚ąíj (y ‚ąí b)j 0 = (a, b) . P1(x) k=0 j=0 ‚ąāxk‚ąíj ‚ąāy j (k ‚ąí j)! j! P2(x) ‚ąí1 P3(x) ex ‚ąí2 ‚ąí4 ‚ąí2 0 2 4 It can be shown that f (x, y) ‚Čą Pn (x, y) when (x, y) is close to (a, b). The higher the degree n, the better the approximation. . .

I Since the formulae above are rather complicated, in this Notes course we consider only Taylor polynomials of degree one I The graph of z = P1 (x, y) is the tangent plane at (a, b) and two. to the surface z = f (x, y); see Topic 2. I The Taylor polynomial of degree one for f (x, y) about (a, b) is I The function P1 (x, y) is a linear combination of 1, x and y. Hence, P1 (x, y) is a polynomial of degree one in two P1 (x, y) = f (a, b) + fx (a, b) (x ‚ąí a) + fy (a, b) (y ‚ąí b). variables x and y. I The Taylor polynomial of degree two is I The function P2 (x, y) is a linear combination of 1, x, y, x2 , xy and y 2 . Therefore, P2 (x, y) is a polynomial of P2 (x, y) = f (a, b) + fx (a, b) (x ‚ąí a) + fy (a, b) (y ‚ąí b) degree two in x and y. 1 + fxx (a, b) (x ‚ąí a)2 2 I In this course, the question ‚ÄúFind the Taylor series + 2fxy (a, b) (x ‚ąí a)(y ‚ąí b) of f (x, y) = ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ about the point (a, b) = ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ up to and  including quadratic terms‚ÄĚ means ‚ÄúFind the Taylor + fyy (a, b) (y ‚ąí b) . polynomial P2 (x, y) of f (x, y) = ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ about the point (a, b) = ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ ‚ÄĚ. . . Example 26 Calculate the Taylor polynomial of degree at most 2 of f (x, y) = ex cos y about (a, b) = (0, 0). Compare the value of f (‚ąí0.1, 0.2) with its Taylor approximation.

P2 (x, y) = 8+32(x‚ąí1)+12(y‚ąí2)+48(x‚ąí1)2 +48(x‚ąí1)(y‚ąí2)+6(y‚ąí2)2 . .   Estimating errors I Recall the linear approximation f (x, y) ‚Čą P1 (x, y) about (a, b) obtained from the first three terms of the Taylor series:

f (x, y) ‚Čą f (a, b) + fx (a, b)(x ‚ąí a) + fy (a, b)(y ‚ąí b).

I Letting h = x ‚ąí a and k = y ‚ąí b, we can write the linear approximation as

f (a + h, b + k) ‚Čą f (a, b) + fx (a, b) h + fy (a, b) k

for (h, k) near (0, 0). 1 I Equivalently, for small changes ‚ąÜx and ‚ąÜy in the values P2 (x, y) = 1 + x + (x2 ‚ąí y 2 ), of x and y, we have f (‚ąí0.1, 0.2) = 0.88680091, P2 (‚ąí0.1, 0.2) = 0.885 f (x + ‚ąÜx, y + ‚ąÜy) ‚Čą f (x, y) + fx (x, y) ‚ąÜx + fy (x, y) ‚ąÜy. . .

I Hence, Example 27 Suppose the temperature T is given by T (x, y) = x2 + y 3 . ‚ąāf ‚ąāf Find the approximate error in T if x = 1, y = 2, ‚ąÜx = 0.1, f (x + ‚ąÜx, y + ‚ąÜy) ‚ąí f (x, y) ‚Čą ‚ąÜx + ‚ąÜy. ‚ąāx ‚ąāy and ‚ąÜy = ‚ąí0.3. I Let ‚ąÜf = f (x + ‚ąÜx, y + ‚ąÜy) ‚ąí f (x, y) denote the change in the value of f arising from the (small) changes ‚ąÜx and ‚ąÜy in the values of x and y. The linear approximation can then be written as ‚ąāf ‚ąāf ‚ąÜf ‚Čą ‚ąÜx + ‚ąÜy. ‚ąāx ‚ąāy I For example, ‚ąÜx and ‚ąÜy might be small errors in some measured values of x and y. The formula lets us estimate the corresponding error ‚ąÜf when we calculate f (x, y). ‚ąí3.4 . .  Maximum possible error Example 28 Suppose the temperature T is given by T (x, y) = x2 + y 3 . I In Example 27, we knew the exact value and sign of the Find the maximum possible errors ‚ąÜx and ‚ąÜy, but usually all we know is the range of 1. absolute error, possible errors. For example, if we know that our 2. relative error, and independent variables can take values x ¬Ī ‚ąÜx 3. percentage error and y ¬Ī ‚ąÜy, then the maximum possible errors in x and y in T if x = 1, y = 2, |‚ąÜx| ‚Č§ 0.1, and |‚ąÜy| ‚Č§ 0.3. are |‚ąÜx| and |‚ąÜy|, respectively. The maximum possible error in the function f (x, y) can then be estimated by

‚ąāf ‚ąāf |‚ąÜf | . |‚ąÜx| + |‚ąÜy|. ‚ąāx ‚ąāy

I Notice that the absolute value symbols mean we always add together non-negative numbers!
